title: Equal-range segmentation with WIDTH_BUCKET
template: WITH customer_sales AS (SELECT orders.customer_id, SUM(order_items.price) AS total_sales FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id GROUP BY orders.customer_id), bounds AS (SELECT MIN(total_sales) AS min_sales, MAX(total_sales) AS max_sales FROM customer_sales), segmented AS (SELECT cs.customer_id, cs.total_sales, WIDTH_BUCKET(cs.total_sales, b.min_sales, b.max_sales + 0.01, 5) AS segment FROM customer_sales cs CROSS JOIN bounds b) SELECT s.segment, MIN(s.total_sales) AS segment_min, MAX(s.total_sales) AS segment_max, COUNT(DISTINCT s.customer_id) AS customers, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS unique_orders FROM segmented s JOIN orders o ON s.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY s.segment ORDER BY s.segment
when_to_use: user asks to create N groups with equal ranges, equal dollar spans, or equal width intervals
histogram-style segmentation where each bucket covers the same value range (e.g. $0-$200, $200-$400, ...)
the question uses words like: equal ranges, equal intervals, equal width, fixed range, range-based buckets
user wants to see how many customers / orders fall in each dollar range
comparing volume in low-value vs high-value buckets with uniform bucket width
anti_patterns: using NTILE for equal-range segmentation — NTILE creates equal-COUNT groups (same number of rows per bucket), not equal-VALUE-RANGE groups; bucket boundaries will be uneven

WIDTH_BUCKET(value, min, max, n) — the upper bound is EXCLUSIVE; the maximum value falls into bucket n+1 (out of range). Always add a small epsilon: WIDTH_BUCKET(value, min_val, max_val + 0.01, n) to keep the maximum value inside bucket n
hard-coding min/max values — always compute them dynamically with a CTE (WITH bounds AS ...) so the ranges adjust automatically when data changes
forgetting CROSS JOIN bounds — the bounds CTE must be joined to every row; omitting this causes a syntax or logic error
notes: WIDTH_BUCKET returns integer bucket numbers starting at 1. Bucket 0 means below the lower bound; bucket n+1 means above the upper bound — both indicate an epsilon miscalculation.
To show the actual value range per bucket use MIN(value) and MAX(value) within each bucket group.
For revenue / spend segmentation the epsilon is typically + 0.01 (cents). For integer metrics like order count use + 1.

title: Equal-count segmentation with NTILE (balanced groups)
intent_tags: same number of customers per group
template: WITH customer_sales AS (SELECT orders.customer_id, SUM(order_items.price) AS total_sales FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id GROUP BY orders.customer_id) SELECT customer_id, total_sales, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY total_sales) AS segment, MIN(total_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY total_sales)) AS segment_min, MAX(total_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY total_sales)) AS segment_max FROM customer_sales ORDER BY total_sales
when_to_use: user asks to split customers into N equal-sized groups (same number of customers in each bucket)
creating quintiles, deciles, quartiles, or percentile-rank tiers
the question uses words like: equal groups, evenly split, same number per group, balanced segments, top 20%, bottom 20%
ranking customers for targeted outreach where you need a fixed number per cohort
anti_patterns: using NTILE for equal-range (histogram) segmentation — NTILE balances row count, not value range; a luxury segment and a budget segment may span very different dollar amounts
expecting NTILE bucket boundaries to be meaningful dollar thresholds — they will not be; use WIDTH_BUCKET when even value intervals matter
using NTILE when you want to describe segments as '$0-$200', '$200-$400' etc — those are equal-range requirements, use WIDTH_BUCKET instead
notes: NTILE(4) = quartiles, NTILE(5) = quintiles, NTILE(10) = deciles.
When the row count is not perfectly divisible by N, NTILE distributes the remainder to the first buckets (they get one extra row each).
NTILE cannot be nested inside another window function in a single SELECT — use a CTE to compute NTILE first, then reference it.

title: Choosing WIDTH_BUCKET vs NTILE: decision guide
template:
-- DECISION: does the user want equal VALUE RANGES or equal GROUP SIZES?
-- Equal ranges (e.g. $0-$200, $200-$400) → WIDTH_BUCKET
--   WITH bounds AS (SELECT MIN(v) AS lo, MAX(v) AS hi FROM t)
--   SELECT WIDTH_BUCKET(v, lo, hi + 0.01, 5) AS bucket FROM t CROSS JOIN bounds
-- Equal sizes (same number of rows per group) → NTILE
--   SELECT NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY v) AS bucket FROM t

-- Full equal-range example:
WITH customer_sales AS (SELECT orders.customer_id, SUM(order_items.price) AS total_sales FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id GROUP BY orders.customer_id), bounds AS (SELECT MIN(total_sales) AS min_sales, MAX(total_sales) AS max_sales FROM customer_sales), segmented AS (SELECT cs.customer_id, cs.total_sales, WIDTH_BUCKET(cs.total_sales, b.min_sales, b.max_sales + 0.01, 5) AS segment FROM customer_sales cs CROSS JOIN bounds b) SELECT s.segment, MIN(s.total_sales) AS segment_min, MAX(s.total_sales) AS segment_max, COUNT(DISTINCT s.customer_id) AS customers, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS unique_orders FROM segmented s JOIN orders o ON s.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY s.segment ORDER BY s.segment
when_to_use: any time user says 'segment', 'bucket', 'group', or 'split' customers/orders/revenue into N groups — first determine WHICH kind of grouping they want
'equal ranges' / 'equal width' / 'equal intervals' / 'equal dollar spans' / 'equal value range' → WIDTH_BUCKET
if the user gives an example like '$0-$200, $200-$400' they want WIDTH_BUCKET
if the user says 'top 20%' or 'bottom quartile' they want NTILE
anti_patterns: defaulting to NTILE for all bucketing requests — NTILE is wrong when the user wants equal dollar/value ranges
defaulting to WIDTH_BUCKET for all bucketing requests — WIDTH_BUCKET is wrong when the user wants the same number of customers per group
using GROUP BY value DIV interval — fragile and does not handle dynamic data ranges
omitting the +0.01 epsilon when using WIDTH_BUCKET — the maximum value always falls into an out-of-range bucket (n+1) without it
notes: Key question to ask internally: does the user care about the value span of each bucket (→ WIDTH_BUCKET) or the count of rows in each bucket (→ NTILE)?
Trigger words for WIDTH_BUCKET: range, interval, span, width, histogram, '$X to $Y'.
Trigger words for NTILE: equal groups, balanced, evenly, quartile, quintile, decile, percentile, top N%.
